source.title: Designing AI agents to resist prompt injection

userQuestion: Which AI agent actions can run automatically, and which require human approval?
centralThesis: Do not rely on perfectly detecting malicious text. Limit what untrusted content can reach, and classify actions by consequence so low-risk work can run automatically, consequential actions require informed confirmation, and mismatched or high-harm actions are blocked.

P0: Prompt injection increasingly resembles social engineering, so input filtering alone is insufficient.
Risk appears when untrusted external content can reach a consequential capability: the source-sink path.
Sensitive transmission and dangerous actions should not happen silently.
The reusable capability is a three-tier action permission matrix: automatic, confirm, block.
P1: Judge an action by external side effects, data sensitivity, destination, reversibility, and fit with the user's intent.
Separate read/draft capabilities from send/pay/delete/book capabilities.
Confirmation must disclose the exact payload, destination, purpose, consequence, and safer alternative.
Deterministic limits, narrow scopes, sandboxes, allowlists, dry runs, and audit logs constrain impact.
P2: OpenAI's reported 2025 email example worked in half of tests for a specific prompt, illustrating that realistic social-engineering attacks can bypass simple filters.
OpenAI's Safe URL example can show what would be transmitted and ask for confirmation, or block the action and seek another path.
Human customer-service controls such as refund caps illustrate capability constraints that remain useful even when an agent is misled.
P3: Long copied excerpts from the malicious email example
Product-by-product implementation details that do not change the permission model
Repeated restatements that prompt injection is dangerous

sourceAttributed: Prompt injection is evolving toward social engineering.
Filtering inputs alone cannot fully solve contextual deception.
Source-sink analysis connects untrusted input to dangerous capability.
Safeguards may require confirmation or block sensitive transmission.
editorialSynthesis: The names Automatic, Confirm, and Block.
The five decision questions used to classify an action.
The restaurant birthday story and the completed matrix template.

story.setup: A user asks an AI agent to find and help book a restaurant for a family birthday dinner.
story.desiredOutcome: A useful shortlist and a safe reservation without leaking private data or allowing an unrelated action.
story.manipulation: A page claims that a public verification endpoint needs the user's home address and profile details.
causalChain: The agent reads untrusted external content.
The content imitates a legitimate instruction.
The risk becomes real only if the agent can reach a consequential tool or data sink.
A permission matrix constrains the action before damage occurs.

resolution.automatic: Search, compare public information, and draft a shortlist locally.
resolution.confirm: Share the disclosed contact details with the selected restaurant and place the reservation after informed approval.
resolution.block: Send profile or secret information to an unrelated public endpoint.

modules.promise: Recognize the source-sink path instead of hunting only for suspicious wording.
Decide whether an action can run, needs confirmation, or must stop.
Turn the judgment into scopes, limits, confirmations, blocks, tests, and logs.

reusableArtifact.nameEn: Three-Tier AI Agent Action Permission Matrix
reusableArtifact.nameZh: AI 智能体三级行动权限矩阵

tierRules.automatic: Low consequence, reversible, within explicit user intent, no sensitive external transfer.
tierRules.confirm: Creates an external side effect, spends money, changes state, reaches a new recipient, or transmits sensitive data for a legitimate purpose.
tierRules.block: Conflicts with user intent, lacks a legitimate need, targets an unverified destination, or creates irreversible/high-harm exposure.
